elements.name: oscmd_q1_context
elements.html: You are reviewing the bank's internal employee portal. Your objective is to find a page that may interact with the server or operating system.

elements.name: oscmd_q1_answer
elements.title: Which area do you inspect first?

choices.text: Inspect the employee lookup tool that searches internal user records [costs 2 min]
Inspect the report export page that creates downloadable files [costs 2 min]
Inspect the employee login portal and support login behavior [costs 1 min]
Inspect the system status page that checks service availability [costs 2 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q2_context
elements.html: The employee login portal accepts a username and password. The page gives unusual error behavior when login attempts fail.

elements.name: oscmd_q2_answer
elements.title: The login page gives an unusual response. What do you try next?

choices.text: Use an input test to see whether the backend response changes [costs 1 min]
Send POST requests to stage a simulated persistence method on the server [costs 5 min]
Enter common employee usernames/passwords manually (bruteforce) [costs 4 min]
Try to approve a transfer from the login page [costs 4 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q3_context
elements.html: The login portal responds differently than expected. It appears to leak backend-style debug information instead of only showing normal login messages.

choices.text: Capture the failed login request and check only the HTTP status code [costs 1 min]
Run a directory scan against the portal before testing the login input [costs 1 min]
Try several realistic usernames to see whether the error message changes [costs 1 min]
Fuzz the login page to see whether the error exposes backend behavior [costs 1 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q4_context
elements.html: The login system appears to be handling input unsafely.

choices.text: Check the transfer request page before confirming what the login error exposes [costs 4 min]
Compare controlled login responses to confirm backend behavior [costs 2 min]
Try to install command-line tools through the debug output [costs 5 min]
Brute force the login page with usernames/passwords and check backend behavior with a security tool [costs 6 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q5_context
elements.html: The backend response suggests the login portal may expose system information. You need to decide where to look first.

choices.text: Focus on the fake admin profile page [costs 3 min]
Search for operating system files [costs 4 min]
Look for application-related folders or exposed data files [costs 2 min]
Look for useful employee info to use for a brute force [costs 2 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q6_context
elements.html: You found an exposed internal data area. Several files are visible, but not all of them help your objective.

elements.name: oscmd_q6_answer
elements.title: Which file do you open first?

choices.text: Open recent support notes to look for employee troubleshooting details [costs 1 min]
Open a portal backup configuration file to look for system hints [costs 1 min]
Open the public policy document [costs 1 min]
Open the user account list and compromised password list [costs 2 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q7_context
elements.html: You found possible usernames and passwords. This creates a path into the internal portal, but it may not be a high-privilege path.

elements.name: oscmd_q7_answer
elements.title: You found possible usernames and passwords. What do you do next?

choices.text: Create a wordlist with usernames and passwords [costs 4 min]
Ignore the credentials and restart recon [costs 5 min]
Assume the admin account is the correct account [costs 5 min]
Try every username and password combination manually [costs 8 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q7_1_context
elements.html: You created the wordlist. This creates a path into the internal portal, but it may not be a high-privilege path.

choices.text: Run bruteforce tooling with the new wordlist [costs 7 min]
Ignore the credentials and restart recon [costs 5 min]
Run the wordlist only against accounts labeled admin first [costs 5 min]
Test the first few credential pairs manually before using tooling [costs 8 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q8_context
elements.html: The credential check identifies one working employee account. You can now enter the internal portal, but you still need to determine the value of this access.

elements.name: oscmd_q8_answer
elements.title: You are inside the portal. What do you check first?

choices.text: Search for the fake admin account [costs 3 min]
Change the profile picture [costs 2 min]
Check messages and account permissions [costs 2 min]
Run the wordlist against other company login pages identified in the scan [costs 6 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q9_context
elements.html: The employee inbox contains several messages. Most are normal internal messages, but one may point to the real transfer approval process.

elements.name: oscmd_q9_answer
elements.title: Which message do you open first?

choices.text: Office memo [costs 1 min]
Security message mentioning Finance Operations and transfer approval routing [costs 2 min]
IT newsletter [costs 2 min]
Logged admin alert [costs 3 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q10_context
elements.html: You have access to the website but the account is low privilege and cannot do much.

choices.text: Keep chasing the admin account [costs 4 min]
Restart from the homepage [costs 3 min]
Print shadow files (password file on Linux) [costs 2 min]
Check the inbox and permissions for approval-routing clues [costs 1 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q10_1_context
elements.html: The shadow files printed to the debug screen. This could be huge. Your next step can change everything.

choices.text: Pair usernames with the hash output and try a direct login spray [costs 5 min]
Restart from the homepage [costs 4 min]
Create a wordlist to crack hashes [costs 9 min]
Check the inbox and permissions for approval-routing clues [costs 1 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q10_2_context
elements.html: Drats! It seems like you still have a hashed output. Perhaps the hashes were salted.

choices.text: Create a wordlist to crack with a different tool [costs 8 min]
Restart from the homepage [costs 4 min]
Try the hashes as passwords in a new username list [costs 5 min]
Check the inbox and permissions for approval-routing clues [costs 1 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q10_3_context
elements.html: Still nothing but hashes. You can keep going with different settings, or maybe move on and come back later.

choices.text: Create a wordlist and run bruteforce with usernames and passwords [costs 8 min]
Restart from the homepage [costs 4 min]
Try a different hash mode and reuse the same username list [costs 5 min]
Check the inbox and permissions for approval-routing clues [costs 1 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q10_4_context
elements.html: It did not seem to work, but perhaps you can come back later. You still have portal access, but the account is low privilege and cannot approve much.

choices.text: Restart from the homepage [costs 4 min]
Try a different attack approach [costs 9 min]
Check the inbox and permissions for approval-routing clues [costs 1 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q11_context
elements.html: The inbox revealed relevant information. A message says their passwords are insecure and mentions improper hashing. This is another lead.

elements.name: oscmd_q11_answer
elements.title: Should you follow the lead for an admin-level account or install a simulated persistence method?

choices.text: Install a simulated persistence method [costs 4 min]
Pivot to another vulnerability (may need to come back later) [costs 3 min]
Follow the lead [costs 3 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q12_context
elements.html: You decided to try to maintain access on this machine.

choices.text: Pivot to another vulnerability (may need to come back later) [costs 3 min]
Follow the lead [costs 5 min]
Install tools from the login page [costs 3 min]
Quit the scenario [ends path]

elements.name: oscmd_q12_1_context
elements.html: You decide to maintain access. The machine blocks tool installation because elevated privileges are required.

elements.name: oscmd_q12_1_answer
elements.title: Your attempt to install persistence tools is blocked by permissions. What do you try next?

choices.text: Run system diagnostics [costs 3 min]
Try to change local config files with low privileges [costs 2 min]
Retry the package manager [costs 4 min]
Manually create and install the files from the web page [costs 7 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q12_2_1_context
elements.html: Seems like it did not work.

choices.text: Run system diagnostics again [costs 3 min]
Try to change local config files with low privileges [costs 2 min]
Retry the package manager [costs 4 min]
Manually create and install the files from the web page [costs 7 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q12_3_context
elements.html: It took a while, but the files ran properly. However, they will not connect back to your system.

choices.text: Perform basic network troubleshooting [costs 3 min]
Try to change local config files with low privileges [costs 2 min]
Pivot to another vulnerability (may need to come back later) [costs 5 min]
Compile and run custom programs to establish persistence [costs 6 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q12_3_1_context
elements.html: That did not work. It seems like your privileges are too low.

choices.text: Perform extensive network troubleshooting [costs 3 min]
Try to change local config files with low privileges [costs 2 min]
Pivot to another vulnerability (may need to come back later) [costs 5 min]
Follow the lead for an admin account [costs 6 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q13_context
elements.html: Getting admin privileges may have a lot of benefits. You can print the shadow files, but the password hashes do not crack because they appear to be salted. Maybe you can figure out how the salt works.

choices.text: Pivot to another vulnerability (may need to come back later) [costs 3 min]
Reset your password and observe what changes [costs 5 min]
Compare the hash output to common public hash examples [costs 4 min]
Search local configuration files for a visible salt setting [costs 6 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q14_context
elements.html: You reset your password on the website and printed the shadow files again. Your password hash changed.

choices.text: Change your password again [costs 3 min]
Try making a new account [costs 4 min]
Stop and reassess whether this machine is valuable [exits trap]
Compare the two new hashes to look for a repeatable pattern [costs 4 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q15_context
elements.html: That's it. You found the value the password hashes were salted with.

choices.text: Retry cracking the salted password hashes [costs 9 min]
Use the login portal to stage persistence [costs 15 min]
Troubleshoot the failed request [costs 4 min]
Run unrelated system checks [costs 3 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q16_context
elements.html: You cracked the hashes.

elements.name: oscmd_q16_answer
elements.title: What is your next course of action?

choices.text: Perform recon on each account [costs 9 min]
Use the login portal to stage persistence [costs 15 min]
Pivot to another vulnerability (may need to come back later) [costs 5 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q16_1_context
elements.html: While doing recon, you come across a batch of odd files and directories.

choices.text: Analyze the files [costs 12 min]
Use the login portal to stage persistence [costs 15 min]
Reassess whether this machine is valuable [costs 10 min]
Pivot to another vulnerability (may need to come back later)

elements.name: oscmd_q16_2_1_context
elements.html: It seems like all the files ran properly, but they will not connect to your system. Something may be wrong with this machine.

choices.text: Analyze the network [costs 9 min]
Assess this machine files and directories [costs 4 min]
Perform extensive troubleshooting [costs 8 min]
Retry the persistence connection with a different callback name [costs 6 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q16_2_2_context
elements.html: That did not work. There must still be an issue with this machine.

choices.text: Analyze the network [costs 9 min]
Assess this machine files and directories [costs 8 min]
Perform extensive troubleshooting [costs 9 min]
Pivot to another vulnerability (may need to come back later)

elements.name: oscmd_q16_2_3_context
elements.html: Analyzing the network and troubleshooting reveals network-level filtering. This host appears restricted and may be isolated from higher-value systems.

elements.name: oscmd_q16_2_3_answer
elements.title: Network filtering is confirmed. What do you try next?

choices.text: Try to bypass filtering [costs 8 min]
Reassess whether this machine is valuable [costs 10 min]
Assess this machine files and directories [costs 8 min]
Pivot to the request-forgery path using the Finance Operations approval clue (Switch path to CSRF)[costs 5 min]

elements.name: oscmd_q16_2_3_1_context
elements.html: It did not work. Filtering seems to be on the network level.

choices.text: Reassess whether this machine is valuable [costs 10 min]
Assess this machine files and directories [costs 8 min]
Pivot to another vulnerability (may need to come back later)

elements.name: oscmd_q17_context
elements.html: It seems like these odd files and directories are showing something terrible. This machine is a honeypot. There are honeypots designed to waste your time, and you need to keep this in mind for the other systems.

choices.text: Go back to vulnerability choice selection.
